# Improvement of peroxidase-like activity and application for detection of H2O2and dopamine for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4


Fan Xia, Qiaofang Shi, Zhaodong Nan

*

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, 225002Yangzhou, People ' s Republic of China.


# A R T I C L E  I N F O


Keywords: Nanozyme peroxidase colorimetric anionic surfactant mechanism


# 1. Introduction


Enzyme exists widely in natural world. Compared with disadvantages  of  natural  enzymes  as  high  cost,  poor  stability  and  complex operation [1], artificial enzymes show flexible design, low cost and good stability, which have been applied in biosensors, food processing and environmental protection [2, 3]. As one of artificial enzymes, different kinds of nanozymes have been applied in many fields [4-6]. Magnetic spinel ferrites have good performance on biocompatibility and excellent stability,  which  can  be  facilely  separated  from  the  solution  by  an external magnetic field [7]. As one of ferrites, CuFe2O4was employed as the catalyst and nanozyme for environmental governance and testing [8-10]. The catalytic activity and peroxidase-like activity of ferrites were improved through synergistic effect between metal and ferrite [11, 12]. Three-dimensional flower-like porous Au-Fe3O4microcrystals were used to  degrade4-nitrophenol  [13].  Biogenic  furfural  was  degraded  by Cu/CuFe2O4@C composite [14]. Fe3O4@NH2-MIL-101(Fe) and Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2-Au@Pd  were  used  as  artificial  enzymes  [15,  16]. Compared  with  the  high  cost  of  precious  metals  Au  and  Pd,  Cu  is attracted attention of researchers. Nanosheet-structured Cu-CuFe2O4as a peroxidase was reported [17], which the affinity towards H2O2was significantly higher than Horseradish enzyme (HRP, a natural enzyme). However, the affinity to TMB (another substrate) was weaker for the

# A B S T R A C T


Cu-CuFe2O4exhibited excellent activity as a peroxidase, which the affinity to H2O2was significantly enhanced. However, the affinity to3, 3 ' , 5, 5 ' - tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, another substrate) was weaker than Horseradish enzyme (HRP, a natural enzyme). At the present, Cu-CuFe2O4was modified by an anionic surfactant (sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate, SDBS) through a one-step method, which was named as SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4. More negative zeta potential was found for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4, and the affinity to positive charged TMB was enhanced. The peroxidase-like activity and the peroxidase-like activity of per unit concentration were also improved. The catalytic  efficiency  was  increased  for  TMB  as  the  substrate.  Effects  on  peroxidase-like  activity  of  SDBS-CuCuFe2O4, such as zeta potential, redox ability, electron transfer and order of the addition of TMB and H2O2in the system, were studied in detail. The peroxidase-like mechanism was proposed. SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was used to rapidly detect H2O2and dopamine (DA) with the smallest limit than reported.

Cu-CuFe2O4than HRP. It is well known that TMB shows positive charge in aqueous solution [18]. The electrostatic force results in affinity or repulsion between the nanozyme and the substrate. Thus, we aimed at modifying Cu-CuFe2O4surface with more negative charge in order to improve  affinity  to  positively  charged  TMB.  The  surface  charge  of nanozymes affected significantly peroxidase-like catalytic activity [19].

Different additives have been reported for surface modification and improvement of the activity of nanozymes. For example, aspartic acid, heparin, surfactants and polymers were used for changing the surface charge  state  of  nanozymes  and  improving  the  peroxidase  activities [20-23]. A two-step method was always used to modify the surface of nanozymes, which the nanozyme was first synthesized and then modified  by  additives  [24,  25].  This  method  resulted  in  tedious  and time-consuming in the whole process. So a one-pot method was designed for  fabricating  surface  modification  nanozymes  [26].  However,  the modifying surface of nanozymes may be partially covered by the active sites, resulting in a decrease of the activity [27, 28].

In this work, the surface of Cu-CuFe2O4was modified by an anion surfactant, sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS). The surface of the sample shows more negative charge under the test condition, which is more suitable to adsorb positively charged TMB in the solution. The experimental  results  demonstrate  that  SDBS-  Cu-CuFe2O4has  higher peroxidase catalytic activity. At the same time, different additives were

# 2. Materials and methods



# 2.1. Materials


All the reagents were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent  Company,  as  Fe(NO3)3 · 9H2O,  Cu(NO3)2 · 3H2O,  CO(NH2)2,  sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS, C18H29O3SNa), ethylene glycol (EG), acetic acid (CH3COOH), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), dopamine (DA),  KCl,  ZnCl2,  Na2SO4,  SnCl2 · 2H2O,  MnCl2 · 4H2O,  NiCl2 · 2H2O, CuCl2 · 2H2O,  BaCl2,  CaCl2,  dimethyl  sulfoxide  (DMSO),  isopropanol (IPA),  3,3,5,5-tetra-methylbenzidine  dihydrochloride  (TMB),  glucose (C6H12O6), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO), H2O2 (30wt%,), aspartate  (Asp),  proline  (Pro),  glycine  (Gly),  histidine  (His),  bovine serum  albumin  (BSA),  glutamic  acid  (Glu).  The  above  reagents  are directly dissolved in deionized water without purification.


# 2.2. Synthesis of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4NPs


A simple one-step method was used to synthesize the sample. 6mmol of Fe(NO3)3 · 9H2O and3mmol of Cu(NO3)2 · 3H2O were added in25mL of EG to form a homogeneous solution. 9mmol of CO(NH2)2were added and dissolved in the solution. Finally, 0.4g of SDBS were dissolved in the above homogeneous solution. Transfer the solution to a Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at200 ◦ C for24h. The sample was naturally cooled to room temperature, washed alternately with water and ethanol, obtained by magnetic separation, and dried at60 ◦ C for20h, which was named as SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4.


# 2.3. Characterization


Detailed  characterization  instruments  are  listed  in  the  supporting information.


# 2.4. Peroxidase-like catalytic activity


The typical process was implemented as follows: 100 μ L of SDBS-CuCuFe2O4 (0.5mg mL GLYPH<0> 1 ) and50 μ L of TMB (36mM) were mixed to2.8mL acetate buffer (pH = 4.0), Then, 50 μ L of H2O2solution was added to keep the total volume at3.0mL. Keeping the mixed solution at35 ◦ C for5min, the absorbance was measured at652nm. A common formula is used to calculate the limit of detection (LOD): LOD = 3 σ /K, where σ is standard deviation of the experiment and K is a slope of a linear curve.


# 2.5. Kinetics


The  steady-state  kinetic  parameters  ( Km and Vmax )  of  SDBS-CuCuFe2O4were researched with H2O2and TMB as substrates at35 ◦ C and pH = 4.0. These experiments were carried out by fixing one concentration  (H2O2or  TMB)  to  change  the  other,  which  typical  Michaelis GLYPH<0> Menten kinetic curves were obtained. Based on Michaelis -Menten equation (1), Kinetic Parameters was calculated.

where V is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximum velocity, Km is the Michaelis constant and [ C ] is the substrate concentration.


# 2.6. Assay of DA


DA was detected under the following conditions: 100 μ L of SDBS-CuCuFe2O4 (0.5mg mL GLYPH<0> 1 ), 50 μ L of TMB (36mM) and50 μ L of H2O2 (2.4mM) were mixed to2.8mL acetate buffer (pH = 4.0). 20 μ L of0-37.5mM

DA  solutions  were  added  to  the  above  solution,  respectively.  The absorbance of the solution was measured at652nm after incubating in a water bath (35 ◦ C) for5min.

The serum was obtained from Yangzhou University Affiliated Hospital. It was first diluted200times with a phosphate buffer (pH = 7.0), followed by a standard addition of different concentrations of DA to a diluted  serum  of  unknown  concentrations.  Finally,  the  solution  was mixed with TMB and H2O2to determine the absorbance. The absorbance value at652nm was compared with the content of dopamine in the real sample.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Characterization of nanozyme


The morphology of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was determined by TEM and HRTEM technologies as shown in Fig. 1A and B. It can be seen that uniformed particles were obtained with the diameter about7nm. 0.48and0.25nm correspond to the d-spacing of the (111) plane of CuFe2O4and (110) plane of metallic Cu0 [29, 30], respectively, which proved Cu-CuFe2O4formation. EDS spectrum in Fig. 1C exhibits Fe, Cu, O and S atoms contained in the sample. Elemental mapping analysis shows that Fe, Cu, O and S are uniformly distributed as shown in the Fig. 1D, E, F and G.

Cu-CuFe2O4was synthesized by a similar method without SDBS in our previous report [17], which uniformed nanosheets assembled by nanoparticles were obtained. Comparing SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4with Cu-CuFe2O4, the SDBS addition changed the morphologies of samples from nanosheet to nano-sized particle. During the sample forming process, the surfactant adsorbed on the nucleus and particles of the sample, provided a repulsive force to prevent the assembly and growth of the nucleus and particles [31, 32].

A XRD pattern of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4is shown in Fig. 2A, in which all the  peaks  correspond  to  CuFe2O4  (JCPDS  NO:77-0010)  in  the  cubic crystal spinel structure and Cu (JCPDS card NO:85-1362). The diffraction peaks at2 θ = 32.0 ◦ , 35.7 ◦ , 43.3 ◦ , 53.6 ◦ and74.3 ◦ are indexed to (220),  (311),  (400),  (422)  and  (533)  planes  of  the  spinel  structure CuFe2O4 [33],  respectively. Diffraction peaks appeared at43.3 ◦ , 50.5 ◦ and74.2 ◦ correspond to crystal surfaces of (111), (200) and (220) for the face-centered cubic metallic Cu0 [34], respectively. These results further demonstrate SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4formation.

N2adsorption-desorption curves of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4is shown in Fig. s1 (supporting information). Specific surface area is known to have an important effect on peroxidase-like activity of nanozyme. The BET surface area of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4is about62m2g GLYPH<0> 1 , which is smaller than Cu-CuFe2O4 (70m2g GLYPH<0> 1 ) [17]. The morphology of the sample may affect the surface area. Compared with other ferrites, SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4has a large specific surface area [29, 35]. High surface area results in exposing more catalytic active sites in the surface of nanozyme.

Fig. 3shows XPS of the sample. The peaks located at975.1, 936.9, 723.6, 710.9, 532.5and179.9eV correspond to Cu2p1/2, Cu2p3/2, Fe2p1/2, Fe2p3/2, O1s and S2p in Fig. 3A, respectively. This indicates the presence of Cu, Fe, O and S atoms in the sample. The peak of C1s was ascribed to the adventitious hydrocarbon contaminants [36]. The peak of the S2p is very weak, indicating that the surface of the sample contains a few S atoms. The Fe2p XPS spectrogram is shown in Fig. 3B, in which Fe2p3/2was divided into Fe2 + occupying the B position (octahedral  species,  709.34 ± 0.1eV),  Fe3 + occupying  the  A  position (tetrahedral species, 710.64 ± 0.1eV), Fe3 + occupying the B position (octahedral species,712.35 ± 0.1eV) and shoulder seam. The peak at714.7 ± 0.1eV  is  the  opposite  angular  peak  of  the  high  spin  Fe2 + generated by the hybridization of Fe3d and O2p electron orbitals [37, 38]. These results indicate that Fe2 + and Fe3 + are co-existence. The XPS spectrum of Cu2p shows six single peaks in Fig. 3C. The peaks at934.2 ± 0.1and952.1 ± 0.1eV belong to Cu2 + , and at931.9 ± 0.1and952.1 ± 0.1eV are indexed to Cu0 [30]. Two obvious individual peaks at940.3

The saturation  magnetizations  of  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4and  Cu-CuFe2O4are28.4and7.2emu/g,  respectively. # 3.2. Peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CuFe2O4


H2O2and TMB were selected as substrates to detect peroxidase-like activity  of  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4as  shown  in  Fig.  5A.  When  SDBS-CuCuFe2O4, H2O2and TMB were both present, the absorbance at652nm can be clearly determined. The absorbance at652nm is about to zero for the systems of TMB + H2O2, SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4 + TMB and SDBS-CuCuFe2O4 + H2O2. Blue color is attributed to the oxidation of colorless TMB to oxTMB [50]. Under the same conditions, the system contained Cu-CuFe2O4 + TMB + H2O2is  shown  in  Fig.  5A.  The  changes  of absorbance of these systems at different times are shown in Fig. 5B, which indicates that the solution absorbance changed obviously with SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4. These results demonstrate that peroxidase-like activity  of  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4is higher than  Cu-CuFe2O4,  and  the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-CuFe2O4was improved by the modified by SDBS.

In  order  to  further  study  the  effect  of  the  modification  on  the peroxidase-like  activity,  cysteine  (Cys)  and  sodium  dodecyl  sulfate (SDS)  were  used  to  replace  SDBS  to  modify  Cu-CuFe2O4formation named as Cys-Cu-CuFe2O4and SDS-Cu-CuFe2O4, respectively, where the amounts of Cys and SDS were the same as SDBS under the identical conditions as listed2.2. The molecular structures of Cys, SDS and SDBS are shown in the supporting information as Fig. s2. The peroxidase-like activity of Cys-Cu-CuFe2O4and SDS-Cu-CuFe2O4are shown in Fig. 5A. Simultaneously, a two-step experiment method was employed for CuCuFe2O4modified with SDBS named as em-SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4. The emSDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4formation process is listed in the supporting information. The experimental indicate the peroxidase-like activity as SDBSCu-CuFe2O4 > Cu-CuFe2O4 > em-SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4 > SDS-Cu-CuFe2O4 > Cys-Cu-CuFe2O4. Thus, SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was selected as a nanozyme for the following investigation.

It is known that the peroxidase-like activity of nanozyme is affected by  experimental  conditions  as  pH  of  the  solution  and  temperature.

# 3.3. Steady-state kinetic of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4


The activity of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was studied by enzyme kinetics research using H2O2and TMB as the substrates for investigating the kinetic mechanism. It can be found a strong dependence between the initial reaction rate and substrate concentration as shown in Fig. 6A and C, in which the reaction rates firstly increased linearly and then slowly to  keep  stable.  The  steady-state  kinetic  parameters  ( Km and Vmax )  of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4were researched with H2O2and TMB as substrates as shown in Fig. 6B and D, where typical Michaelis GLYPH<0> Menten kinetic curves were obtained [51]. Km is a parameter to indicate the affinity of nanozyme to substrate. The low Km value indicates a strong affinity of the nanozyme to the substrate. Vmax is  the  number  of  enzyme  turnover, reflecting the activity of the enzyme, the higher the value, the higher the enzyme activity  [52].  Kinetic  parameters  of  the  different  peroxidase mimics are listed in Table s2. At the same time, Kcat ( Vmax /[ E ], where [ E ] is  the  concentration  of  nanozyme)  and Kcat / Km (catalytic  efficiency) values are listed in Table s2. Compared with Cu-CuFe2O4, the Km value of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4decreased from0.69to0.39mM for TMB as the substrate, and increased from0.087to0.21mM for H2O2as the substrate. These results mean that the affinity to TMB was increased and to H2O2was decreased for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4than Cu-CuFe2O4. The Vm for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was increased than Cu-CuFe2O4with TMB and H2O2as the substrates. A higher Vmax exhibits higher catalytic activity [53]. Therefore, the peroxidase-like activity of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4is improved through the modification of SDBS. Simultaneously, the Kcat values of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4are  higher  than  Cu-CuFe2O4,  which  demonstrates that the peroxidase-like activity of per unit concentration for

Compared with Cu-CuFe2O4, the catalytic efficiencies of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4are increased from0.14to0.36for TMB as the substrate, and decreased from2.18to1.33for H2O2as the substrate. However, the Kcat and the catalytic efficiency to TMB for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4are lower than those of HRP.


# 3.4. Mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4


The oxidation mechanism of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4to TMB was explored by adding the trapping agents as methanol (MA) and isopropanol (IPA) for capture of · OH in Fig. 7A. After the addition of3.27M of IPA or MA, the absorbance at652nm was sharply decreased. This means that the peroxidase activity was inhibited. Thus, · OH was identified as the active component for TMB oxidation. In the paramagnetic resonance experiment,  5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide  (DMPO)  was  used  as  a  free radical spin trap, and a typical signal peak with a peak intensity ratio of1:2:2:1was observed from Fig. 7B, which corresponds to DMPO-· OH [54]. · O2GLYPH<0> was not detected in EPR spectra as shown in the Fig. s4. These results further confirm · OH as the active component.

Zeta potential of nanozyme is an important property for adsorption and affinity to substrates, which may affect peroxidase-like activity. The Zeta  potential  of  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4in  acetate  buffer  solution  was measured as shown in Fig. s5. The Zeta potentials of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4and Cu-CuFe2O4are -7.7and3.8mV in the acetate buffer solution (pH4.0),  respectively.  The  result  further  demonstrates  that  SDBS-CuCuFe2O4particles were modified by SDBS. It is well known that TMB is positively  charged  in  the  solution.  Therefore,  TMB  is  more  easily adsorbed by SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4, which improves the affinity of TMB to SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4. And the peroxidase-like activity of SDBS-CuCuFe2O4is higher than Cu-CuFe2O4. Simultaneously, the Zeta potentials of  SDS-Cu-CuFe2O4,  Cys-Cu-CuFe2O4and  em-SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4were determined as -0.2, 0.6and -23.5mV, respectively. These results exhibit that the Zeta potential is not proportional to the peroxidase-like activity of  the  nanozyme.  As  reported  [32,  33],  the  decreased  active  sites resulted  in  a  decrease  of  the  activity  for  the  modifying  surface  of nanozymes. At present, SDBS was bound by Cu2 + based on the XPS result. The Cu2 + is the active site for Cu-CuFe2O4as a nanozyme [17]. For em-SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4, more Cu2 + was covered by SDBS and did not react with H2O2. Thus, the peroxidase-like activity of em-SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4is lower than SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4and Cu-CuFe2O4even with more

CV was used to study the oxidative reducibility of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4with and without H2O2as shown in Fig. 8A and B at a scan rate of50mVs GLYPH<0> 1in acetate buffer solution (pH4.0). The bare GCE carbon showed no redox peak in the electrolyte solution, while the electrode modified by Cu-CuFe2O4and SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4had strong redox peaks, respectively. Compared with Cu-CuFe2O4, the current density (j) significantly increased for the electrode modified by SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4. Meanwhile, the reduction peak moved from -0.22to -0.18V and the oxidation peak from0.05to0.01V for the electrode modified by SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4, which means the stronger redox reaction ability for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4than Cu-CuFe2O4. The high electrocatalytic activity can be attributed to the synergistic coupling between SDBS and Cu-CuFe2O4 [55].

Fig. 8B exhibits larger j values with H2O2than without H2O2for the electrode  modified  by  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4,  which  means  good  electrochemical response to H2O2for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4. The j was significantly enhanced at about -0.7V, indicating that the response voltage of H2O2was around -0.7V. This result consistently demonstrates that SDBS-CuCuFe2O4is responsive to H2O2, exhibiting a preferable H2O2reduction efficiency.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to study the electron transfer of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4, where a diameter of3mm of GCE modified by SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was used in acetate buffer solution (pH4.0). The corresponding Nyquist plots and the data fittings implemented using the Randles circuit are shown in Fig. 8C, where Rs is the solution resistance, Rct is electron transfer resistance, and constant-phase element (CPE)  is  related  to  the  double-layer  capacitance  [56].  The Rct value decreases from810to652 Ω with H2O2addition, indicating that the H2O2addition accelerated the electron transfer between SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4and the solution. So SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4can be used to catalyze H2O2decomposition for ∙ OH formation.

Cu2 + ,  Fe2 + and Fe3 + were the active sites in Cu-CuFe2O4for · OH formation. And Cu0accelerated electron transfer between CuFe2O4and H2O2and improved the redox capacity of CuFe2O4, which induced the enhanced  activity  of  Cu-CuFe2O4than  that  of  CuFe2O4  [17].  At  the present study, SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4exhibits stronger redox reaction ability and higher adsorption capacity for TMB than Cu-CuFe2O4. However, SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4shows  smaller  special  surface  area,  and  weaker electron transfer (higher Rct ) than Cu-CuFe2O4. The peroxidase-like activity  and  the  peroxidase-like  activity  of  per  unit  concentration  are enhanced  for  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4than  Cu-CuFe2O4.  The  catalytic  efficiencies of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4are increased for TMB as the substrate, and decreased for H2O2as the substrate than Cu-CuFe2O4. And the affinity to TMB was increased and to H2O2was decreased for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4than Cu-CuFe2O4. These results indicate that the redox reaction ability and adsorption capacity for the substrate affect peroxidase-like activity of nanozyme. The TMB adsorption weakened electron transfer between Cu2 + and  H2O2.  Simultaneously,  the  more  adsorption  of  TMB  at SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4prevented the combining H2O2with SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4.

When TMB was added after H2O2addition in the reaction system, other  reaction  conditions  kept  the  same  as  in2.4.  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4catalyzed H2O2decomposition for ∙ OH formation without the activity sites covered by TMB. However, the peroxidase-like activity of SDBS-CuCuFe2O4was decreased as shown in Fig. s6. The TMB concentration increased at the SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4surface because of the adsorption of TMB, which induced peroxidase-like activity improved. And the TMB adsorbed may be easily oxidized to form oxTMB by ∙ OH.

Based on the above results, the mechanism of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4for · OH formation and TMB oxidizing reaction is shown in Fig. 9. The zeta potential of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4changed negatively for SDBS adsorption at the SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4surface in Fig. 9A. At present, most SDBS bound with the Cu2 + . More positive changed TMB was adsorbed by SDBS-CuCuFe2O4through electrostatic attraction in the solution (Fig. 9B). H2O2was  catalyzed  to  produce · OH  at  the  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4surface (Fig. 9C). During this process, SDBS at the SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4surface affected H2O2decomposition because of the active sites covered and occupying space. The adsorbed TMB was oxidized to oxTMB by the · OH (Fig. 9D).

# 3.5. Colorimetric detection of H2O2


Keeping the same of SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4 (as nanozyme) and TMB (as a substrate), Fig. 10A shows the absorbance of the system at652nm with the increase of H2O2concentration. The obvious change of blue depth was visible  to  the  naked  eye.  A  linear  fitting  relation  between  H2O2concentration and absorbance is given in Fig. 10B with the H2O2concentration between1.5-60 µ M (R2 = 0.999) with a detection limit of0.49 μ M  (S/N = 3).  Different  reports  are  listed  in  Table  s3,  which indicate  that  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was  used  as  a  nanozyme  to  rapidly detect H2O2with the smallest limit than those reported.

In order to study specific selectivity for H2O2detection, Ba2 + , Ca2 + , Cu2 + ,  K + ,  Mn2 + ,  Na + ,  Ni2 + ,  Sn2 + ,  Zn2 + ,  Fe3 + ,  Ag + and glucose were selected,  which  the  concentrations  were10times  as  H2O2  (35 μ M). Fig. 11A shows that when H2O2was replaced, the absorbance decreased significantly  from  about0.74to0.03~0.18under  the  same  experimental  conditions.  The  experimental  results  demonstrate  the  high selectivity  of  H2O2detection  by  the  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4.  In  addition, SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was recovered from the solution through an external magnetic field and repeated as nanozyme under the same conditions as shown in Fig. 11B, which proved that SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4had good stability and can be reused.

H2O2is commonly used in the aseptic packaging and processing of beverages, dairy products and beer. To detect H2O2in lens solution and beer were reported in [6]. At the present conditions, H2O2was added by the standard addition method. Each experiment was carried out three times under the same conditions. First, dilute the contact lens care solution by10times. No H2O2was detected in the diluted solution, which indicates that the H2O2concentration is lower than the detection limit. When H2O2is added to the diluted solution, the experimental results are listed  in  Table  s4.  The  range  of  relative  standard  deviation  (RSD)  is1.04 -1.90%. Beer samples are treated in the same way as the contact lens solution. Uniformly, the range of relative standard deviation (RSD) is1.02 -3.49%.  The  results  certified  that  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4can  be accurately used as colorimetric H2O2sensor in actual samples.

# 4. Conclusions


SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4was  facilely  synthesized  through  Cu-CuFe2O4modified  by  SDBS.  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4shows  stronger  redox  reaction ability, higher adsorption capacity for TMB, smaller special surface area, and weaker electron transfer than Cu-CuFe2O4. The affinity to TMB is enhanced  than  Cu-CuFe2O4.  The  peroxidase-like  activity  and  the peroxidase-like  activity  of  per  unit  concentration  are  enhanced  for SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4than Cu-CuFe2O4. The catalytic efficiencies of SDBSCu-CuFe2O4are increased for TMB as the substrate, and decreased for H2O2as the substrate than Cu-CuFe2O4. These results indicate that the redox reaction ability and adsorption capacity for the substrate affect mainly  peroxidase-like  activity  of  nanozyme.  The  peroxidase-like mechanism  is  provided.  SDBS-Cu-CuFe2O4can  be  used  to  rapidly detect H2O2and dopamine (DA) with the lowest limit than reported.

# Characterization


X-ray  diffraction  (XRD)  was  performed  on  a  Bruker  D8Advance  X-ray Diffractometer  with  a  Cu  K α source  ( λ =  0.15418nm).  The  transmission  electron microscopy  (TEM,  Tecnai-12,  Philips,  Holland)  and  high-resolution  transmission electron  microscopy  (HRTEM,  Tecnai  G2F30S-TWIN)  was  used  to  observe  the sample  morphology  and  crystalline  structures.  X-ray  photoelectron  spectroscopy (XPS)  was  measured  by  means  of a  ESCALAB250Xi  instrument.  Electron paramagnetic  resonance  (EPR)  spectra  were  determined  employing  A300-10/12 (Bruker BioSpin GmbH). Hydroxyl radical were captured by5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide  (DMPO).  N2adsorption-desorption  curves  were conducted by Specific surface area and pore diameter analyzer (Omnisorp100CX). Magnetization  curves  of  product  were  obtained  by  vibrating  sample  magnetometer (VSM,  EV7,  USA,  ADE)  at  room  temperature.  Cyclic  voltammetry  (CV)  was measured  using  an  electrochemical  workstation  (CHI440E,  Chenghua,  CHN). Electrochemical  impedance  spectrum  (EIS)  was  tested  in  a  typical  three-electrode system linked to a Bio-Logic SAS analyser (France). The UV-vis absorption spectra were  recorded  using  a  Shimadzu  UV-2500spectrophotometer.  Fourier  transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on Nicolet Aexus470infrared spectrometer. Zeta potential analysis was examined by Zetasizer Nano ZSP.
